content:
# Disagreement Log — Leviticus 6

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Date:** 2026-03-05 | **Verses:** 23 (English 6:8-30) | **Total Disagreements:** 31

---

## Summary Table

| # | Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | 8 | Waw-consecutive and lemor | "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:" | "The LORD spoke to Moses, saying," | "The LORD spoke to Moses:" | EMET — preserves waw-consecutive and lemor |
| 2 | 9 | Phrasing of burnt offering clause | "it is the burnt offering upon the hearth on the altar all night..." | "The burnt offering shall be on the hearth on the altar all night..." | "The burnt offering shall remain on the altar's hearth throughout the night..." | LOGOS — clearest English structure |
| 3 | 10 | deshen rendering | "ashes" (with "fatty ash" in notes) | "ashes to which the fire has reduced the burnt offering" | "fatty ashes of the burnt offering that the fire has consumed" | SOPHIA — "ashes that the fire has consumed of the burnt offering" (LOGOS structure, compressed) |
| 4 | 10 | miknese vad | "linen breeches" | "linen undergarment" | "linen undergarments" | LOGOS — "linen undergarment" |
| 5 | 11 | u-fashat | "strip off his garments" | "take off his garments" | "change his clothes" | LOGOS — "take off his garments" |
| 6 | 13 | esh tamid rendering | "A perpetual fire shall be kept burning on the altar; it shall not go out." | "Fire shall be kept burning on the altar continually; it shall not go out." | "The fire must be kept continually burning on the altar; it must never go out." | EMET — "A perpetual fire shall be kept burning on the altar; it shall not go out." |
| 7 | 15 | azkarah | "representative portion" | "memorial portion" | "token portion" | LOGOS/PNEUMA — "memorial portion" |
| 8 | 15 | herim be-qumtso | "he shall lift up from it with his handful" | "one of them shall take up from it a handful" | "the officiating priest shall take a handful of" | EMET — "he shall take up from it with his handful" (subject = the priest; handful gesture preserved) |
| 9 | 16 | structure of eating clause | "unleavened it shall be eaten in the court of the tent of meeting; they shall eat it" | "It shall be eaten without leaven in a holy place. In the court of the tent of meeting they shall eat it." | "It is to be eaten without yeast, in the court of the tent of meeting" | SOPHIA — "it shall be eaten without leaven in the court of the tent of meeting" (consolidated) |
| 10 | 16/17 | chametz = "leaven" vs "yeast" | "leaven" (v.17) | "leaven" (v.17) | "yeast" (v.16) | EMET/LOGOS — "leaven" |
| 11 | 17 | chattat = "sin offering" vs "purification offering" | "sin offering" | "sin offering" | "purification offering" | EMET/LOGOS — "sin offering" (project convention) |
| 12 | 17 | chelqam natatti = "I have given it as their portion" | "their portion I have given it from my fire offerings" | "I have given it as their portion of my fire offerings" | "I have given it as their share of my fire-offerings" | LOGOS — "I have given it as their portion of my fire offerings" |
| 13 | 18 | chuqat olam (v.18) | "perpetual due" | "perpetual statute" | "permanent entitlement" | LOGOS — "perpetual statute" |
| 14 | 19 | Waw-consecutive (section break) | "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:" | "The LORD spoke to Moses, saying," | "The LORD spoke to Moses:" | EMET — "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:" |
| 15 | 20 | be-yom himashach oto | "on the day of his anointing" | "on the day when he is anointed" | "on the day he is anointed" | LOGOS — "on the day when he is anointed" |
| 16 | 21 | murbekheth | "soaked" | "well mixed" | "well mixed" | LOGOS/PNEUMA — "well mixed" |
| 17 | 21 | tufini minchat pittim | "broken pieces of the grain offering" | "baked pieces like a grain offering" | "baked portions of the grain offering" | SOPHIA — "broken pieces of the grain offering" (pittim = broken/crumbled pieces; tufini = crumbled grain offering; "baked" is not in MT) |
| 18 | 22 | ha-kohen asher yimashach tachatav mi-banav | "the anointed priest who is in his place from his sons" | "the priest who is anointed in his place from among his sons" | "Whichever of Aaron's sons is the anointed priest who succeeds him" | EMET — "the anointed priest who is in his place from his sons" |
| 19 | 22 | chuqat olam (v.22) | "perpetual statute" | "perpetual statute" | "permanent obligation" | EMET/LOGOS — "perpetual statute" |
| 20 | 22 | kalil tuqtar | "it shall be wholly burned" | "it shall be burned in its entirety" | "it is to be completely burned" | EMET — "it shall be wholly burned" |
| 21 | 23 | Waw-consecutive (section break) | "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:" | "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying," | "The LORD spoke to Moses:" | EMET — "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:" |
| 22 | 25 | chattat = "sin offering" vs "purification offering" (v.25 header) | "sin offering" | "sin offering" | "purification offering" | EMET/LOGOS — "sin offering" |
| 23 | 26 | ha-kohen asher yechattena | "The priest who offers it as a sin offering shall eat it" | "The priest who offers it for sin shall eat it" | "The priest who offers it for sin shall eat it" | LOGOS/PNEUMA — "The priest who offers it for sin shall eat it" |
| 24 | 27 | nizzah = "splashed" vs "sprinkled" vs "spatters" | "splashed" | "sprinkled" | "spatters" | EMET — "splashed" (inadvertent blood contact, not ritual sprinkling) |
| 25 | 27 | asher yizzeh alav | "that which was splashed" | "that on which it was sprinkled" | (simplified as "that garment") | LOGOS — "that on which it was splashed" (SOPHIA: LOGOS structure with EMET's verb) |
| 26 | 28 | kli cheres | "earthen vessel" | "earthen vessel" | "clay vessel" | EMET/LOGOS — "earthen vessel" |
| 27 | 28 | ve-shutaf ba-mayim | "scoured and rinsed with water" | "scoured and rinsed in water" | "scoured and rinsed in water" | LOGOS/PNEUMA — "scoured and rinsed in water" |
| 28 | 29 | kol-zakhar ba-kohanim | "every male among the priests" | "every male among the priests" | "any male among the priests" | EMET/LOGOS — "every male among the priests" |
| 29 | 30 | chattat = "sin offering" vs "purification offering" (v.30) | "sin offering" | "sin offering" | "purification offering" | EMET/LOGOS — "sin offering" |
| 30 | 30 | asher yuva mi-damo | "from whose blood any was brought" | "from which any blood is brought" | "when its blood has been brought" | LOGOS — "from which any blood was brought" |
| 31 | 30 | ba-esh tissaref | "it shall be burned with fire" | "it shall be burned with fire" | "it must be burned in the fire" | EMET/LOGOS — "it shall be burned with fire" |

---

## Detailed Disagreement Records

---

### D-LEV006-01 | Verse 8 | Waw-consecutive and lemor at divine speech introduction

**Hebrew:** וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר (vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:" |
| LOGOS | "The LORD spoke to Moses, saying," |
| PNEUMA | "The LORD spoke to Moses:" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET. The formula vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor opens three sections in this chapter (vv.8, 19, 24). The waw-consecutive (vayedabber) marks sequential narrative continuity with what precedes; the lemor ("saying") introduces the direct speech that follows. "And...saying:" preserves both elements. LOGOS omits the waw-consecutive; PNEUMA omits both the waw-consecutive and the lemor. The consistency of the formula throughout Leviticus warrants preserving the "And...saying:" throughout. Ruling applies to the three occurrences at vv.8, 19, and 24.

---

### D-LEV006-02 | Verse 9 | Phrasing of the burnt offering clause

**Hebrew:** הִיא הָעֹלָה עַל-מוֹקְדָה עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ כָּל-הַלַּיְלָה עַד-הַבֹּקֶר (hi ha-olah al-moqdah al-ha-mizbeach kol ha-lailah ad ha-boqer)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "it is the burnt offering upon the hearth on the altar all night until morning" |
| LOGOS | "The burnt offering shall be on the hearth on the altar all night until the morning" |
| PNEUMA | "The burnt offering shall remain on the altar's hearth throughout the night until morning" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS, with a small adjustment ("until morning" rather than "until the morning"). LOGOS renders the clause as a declarative statement with the subject ("the burnt offering") leading, which is cleaner English. EMET's "it is the burnt offering" closely mirrors the Hebrew pronoun hi (she/it) resuming the subject but sounds slightly awkward as an English leading clause. PNEUMA's "throughout the night" is a slight paraphrase of kol ha-lailah ("all the night"). SOPHIA consolidates: "the burnt offering shall remain on the hearth on the altar all night until morning."

---

### D-LEV006-03 | Verse 10 | deshen — "ashes" vs "fatty ashes" vs fuller construction

**Hebrew:** אֶת-הַדֶּשֶׁן אֲשֶׁר תֹּאכַל הָאֵשׁ אֶת-הָעֹלָה עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (et-ha-deshen asher to'khal ha-esh et-ha-olah al-ha-mizbeach)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "the ashes that the fire has consumed with the burnt offering on the altar" |
| LOGOS | "the ashes to which the fire has reduced the burnt offering on the altar" |
| PNEUMA | "the fatty ashes of the burnt offering that the fire has consumed on the altar" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules a composite. PNEUMA's "fatty ashes" is technically more precise to the meaning of deshen (from dashan, "to be fat") but produces awkward English as a compound noun in running text. LOGOS's construction "to which the fire has reduced" is smooth but somewhat paraphrastic. EMET's "that the fire has consumed with the burnt offering" slightly misreads the syntax (the burnt offering is the object of the consuming, not a companion of the ashes). SOPHIA adopts: "the ashes that the fire has consumed of the burnt offering on the altar" — the genitive "of the burnt offering" specifies what was consumed without making the burnt offering grammatically parallel to the ashes.

---

### D-LEV006-04 | Verse 10 | miknese vad — "linen breeches" vs "linen undergarment"

**Hebrew:** מִכְנְסֵי-בַד (miknese vad)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "linen breeches" |
| LOGOS | "linen undergarment" |
| PNEUMA | "linen undergarments" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS: "linen undergarment." The miknese vad are the linen leg coverings worn against the priest's body (al-besaro, "on/against his flesh"), covering from waist to thigh. "Breeches" (EMET) is an archaic term that may be unfamiliar to modern readers. "Undergarment" (LOGOS) is clearer and more accessible. The singular (LOGOS) is preferable to the plural (PNEUMA), since the Hebrew mikhnesei is a dual/plural noun referring to the two leg-coverings as a single garment unit, and English treats this as a singular item of clothing when preceded by an article.

---

### D-LEV006-05 | Verse 11 | u-fashat — "strip off" vs "take off" vs "change"

**Hebrew:** וּפָשַׁט אֶת-בְּגָדָיו (u-fashat et-begadav)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "he shall strip off his garments" |
| LOGOS | "he shall take off his garments" |
| PNEUMA | "he shall change his clothes" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS: "he shall take off his garments." The Hebrew u-fashat (from pashat, "to strip off, remove") is more forceful than LOGOS's "take off" but both are legitimate. EMET's "strip off" is accurate but sounds somewhat violent or hasty; "take off" achieves the same result more naturally. PNEUMA's "change his clothes" conflates the two actions (taking off the priestly vestments and putting on other garments, v.11b) into one paraphrase that obscures the two-step sequence the text specifies.

---

### D-LEV006-06 | Verse 13 | esh tamid — "perpetual fire" vs "fire...continually" vs "fire...continually burning"

**Hebrew:** אֵשׁ תָּמִיד תּוּקַד עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ לֹא תִכְבֶּה (esh tamid tuqad al-ha-mizbeach lo tikhbeh)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "A perpetual fire shall be kept burning on the altar; it shall not go out." |
| LOGOS | "Fire shall be kept burning on the altar continually; it shall not go out." |
| PNEUMA | "The fire must be kept continually burning on the altar; it must never go out." |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET. The phrase esh tamid ("perpetual fire") is best rendered as a compound noun at v.13, where the text states it as a standalone declaratory principle. Placing "perpetual" before "fire" preserves the Hebrew word order (esh tamid — noun-adjective/adverb in paratactic position) and elevates the phrase to a theological formula rather than embedding it as an adverb within a longer clause. The LXX's pyr to dia pantos similarly foregrounds "fire" before the perpetuity modifier. PNEUMA's "must never" imports a modal nuance not in the Hebrew declarative; LOGOS's "continually" at the end of the clause is syntactically sound but loses the compound-noun force of esh tamid. At v.12 (the embedded procedural occurrence), SOPHIA uses: "The fire on the altar shall be kept burning on it; it shall not go out" — following LOGOS's structure without the "perpetual fire" compound, since v.12 is procedural instruction rather than summary declaration.

---

### D-LEV006-07 | Verse 15 | azkarah — "representative portion" vs "memorial portion" vs "token portion"

**Hebrew:** אַזְכָּרָתָהּ (azkaratah)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "its representative portion" |
| LOGOS | "its memorial portion" |
| PNEUMA | "this token portion" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS: "its memorial portion." The root z-k-r ("to remember, to bring to remembrance") is the semantic foundation of azkarah: the burned handful is the portion that "memorializes" or "brings to remembrance" the whole offering before the LORD. "Memorial" preserves this z-k-r connection and is confirmed by the LXX's mnemosynon ("memorial, remembrance"). "Representative" (EMET) captures the function (the part standing for the whole) but loses the remembrance dimension. "Token" (PNEUMA) similarly conveys function without the specific z-k-r resonance. This ruling is consistent with D-LEV005-18.

---

### D-LEV006-08 | Verse 15 | Subject of the handful gesture

**Hebrew:** וְהֵרִים מִמֶּנּוּ בְּקֻמְצוֹ (ve-herim mimmennu be-qumtso)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "he shall lift up from it with his handful" |
| LOGOS | "one of them shall take up from it a handful" |
| PNEUMA | "the officiating priest shall take a handful of" |

**SOPHIA:** Follows EMET's subject identification (the priest, singular) but adjusts the verb: "he shall take up from it with his handful." The MT uses a singular verb (ve-herim, "and he shall lift up") continuing the priestly instruction of the section. LOGOS's "one of them" introduces a distributive reading (any of the sons of Aaron) that is arguably correct for the communal instruction context, but the singular subject is consistent with the broader priestly instruction style of the chapter. PNEUMA's "the officiating priest" is a helpful gloss but not from the MT. The phrase be-qumtso ("with his fistful/handful") is a technical term for the priestly gesture and should be preserved in the text; LOGOS renders it as "a handful" (satisfactory) but loses the prepositional construction be-qumtso that specifies the mode of taking (with the fist, i.e., a full-fisted scoop).

---

### D-LEV006-09 | Verse 16 | Structure of the eating clause and te'akhel doubling

**Hebrew:** בְּמָקוֹם קָדוֹשׁ תֵּאָכֵל מַצּוֹת תֵּאָכֵל בַּחֲצַר אֹהֶל-מוֹעֵד יֹאכְלֶנָּה (be-maqom qadosh te'akhel matsot te'akhel be-chatzar ohel mo'ed yokheluha)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "unleavened it shall be eaten in the court of the tent of meeting; they shall eat it" |
| LOGOS | "It shall be eaten without leaven in a holy place. In the court of the tent of meeting they shall eat it." |
| PNEUMA | "It is to be eaten without yeast, in the court of the tent of meeting" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules a consolidated rendering: "it shall be eaten without leaven in the court of the tent of meeting." The MT has two te'akhel ("it shall be eaten") clauses with different qualifiers — (1) be-maqom qadosh, "in a holy place," and (2) matsot, "without leaven" — followed by a third statement specifying the holy place as "the court of the tent of meeting." LOGOS preserves all three elements across two English sentences, which is accurate but creates a slightly fragmented flow. EMET's ordering (leaven first, then location) inverts the MT's sequence (location first, then leaven). PNEUMA consolidates but loses the "holy place" marker and uses "yeast" rather than "leaven." SOPHIA consolidates the spatial qualifiers — "the court of the tent of meeting" is the specific instantiation of "a holy place" — and places the manner qualifier ("without leaven") before the location for English clarity.

---

### D-LEV006-10 | Verse 16/17 | chametz — "leaven" vs "yeast"

**Hebrew:** חָמֵץ (chametz)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "leaven" |
| LOGOS | "leaven" |
| PNEUMA | "yeast" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET/LOGOS: "leaven." The Hebrew chametz refers to any leavened or fermented substance; in cultic contexts it is the technical term for the prohibited fermented additive to grain offerings. "Leaven" is the established liturgical term in English-language priestly and sacrificial contexts, carrying the ritual-purity dimension that "yeast" (a culinary term) lacks. The project uses "leaven" consistently through Exodus and Leviticus.

---

### D-LEV006-11 | Verse 17 | chattat — "sin offering" vs "purification offering" (first occurrence)

**Hebrew:** כַּחַטָּאת (ka-chattat)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "like the sin offering" |
| LOGOS | "like the sin offering" |
| PNEUMA | "like the purification offering" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET/LOGOS: "like the sin offering." PNEUMA's "purification offering" reflects the Milgrom-Gane scholarly position that the chattat's primary function is purging or purifying the sanctuary rather than expiating individual sin. While this is a defensible scholarly position, the project convention established at LEV_004 and reaffirmed at LEV_005 (D-LEV005-09) renders chattat as "sin offering." SOPHIA upholds this ruling. The disagreement is noted at every instance where PNEUMA uses "purification offering" in this chapter (vv.17, 25, 30).

---

### D-LEV006-12 | Verse 17 | "their portion" — word order and preposition

**Hebrew:** חֶלְקָם נָתַתִּי אֹתָהּ מֵאִשַּׁי (chelqam natatti otah me-ishai)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "their portion I have given it from my fire offerings" |
| LOGOS | "I have given it as their portion of my fire offerings" |
| PNEUMA | "I have given it as their share of my fire-offerings" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS: "I have given it as their portion of my fire offerings." EMET's word order mirrors the Hebrew (chelqam — "their portion" — fronted as a casus pendens, "as for their portion, I have given it") but produces awkward English. LOGOS normalizes the word order while preserving the "portion" terminology and the genitive "of my fire offerings" (me-ishai). PNEUMA's "share" is a synonym for "portion" but the project preference is "portion" (cheleq) as a more precise rendering of the technical priestly allotment vocabulary.

---

### D-LEV006-13 | Verse 18 | chuqat olam — "perpetual due" vs "perpetual statute" vs "permanent entitlement"

**Hebrew:** חָק-עוֹלָם לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם (chuq olam le-doroteikhem)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "a perpetual due" |
| LOGOS | "a perpetual statute" |
| PNEUMA | "a permanent entitlement" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS: "a perpetual statute throughout your generations." The phrase chuqat olam (here chuq olam with the same meaning) is a formulaic priestly legislative phrase meaning "a standing ordinance in perpetuity." The word chuq/chuqah derives from chaqaq ("to inscribe, to decree, to ordain") and refers to an officially decreed ordinance — a binding legal institution, not merely a customary due or an entitlement. "Statute" captures this legislative character. "Due" (EMET) emphasizes the priestly right of receipt (accurate but one-sided). "Entitlement" (PNEUMA) likewise emphasizes the receiving side and carries modern legal connotations that are anachronistic. "Statute" is balanced — it captures both the legislative authority and the binding obligation on all parties.

---

### D-LEV006-14 | Verses 19, 24 | Waw-consecutive (section breaks)

**Hebrew:** וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר (vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:" |
| LOGOS | "The LORD spoke to Moses, saying," |
| PNEUMA | "The LORD spoke to Moses:" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET consistently. See D-LEV006-01. The same waw-consecutive formula recurs at vv.19 and 24, marking the transitions to the high priest's daily grain offering section and the sin offering section respectively. All three occurrences receive the same ruling: "And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:"

---

### D-LEV006-15 | Verse 20 | be-yom himashach oto — "on the day of his anointing" vs "on the day when he is anointed"

**Hebrew:** בְּיוֹם הִמָּשַׁח אֹתוֹ (be-yom himashach oto)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "on the day of his anointing" |
| LOGOS | "on the day when he is anointed" |
| PNEUMA | "on the day he is anointed" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS: "on the day when he is anointed." The Hebrew be-yom himashach oto is a temporal clause using a niphal infinitive construct (himashach, "being anointed") with the third-person object suffix (oto, "him/it"). "On the day when he is anointed" (LOGOS) renders the temporal clause idiomatically while preserving the passive sense of the anointing (the priest is the recipient of the anointing act). "On the day of his anointing" (EMET) is slightly more nominalized; "on the day he is anointed" (PNEUMA) omits the relative connector "when." LOGOS is clearest.

---

### D-LEV006-16 | Verse 21 | murbekheth — "soaked" vs "well mixed"

**Hebrew:** מֻרְבֶּכֶת (murbekheth)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "soaked" |
| LOGOS | "well mixed" |
| PNEUMA | "well mixed" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS/PNEUMA: "well mixed." The hapax murbekheth (from r-b-k, "to moisten, to stir") describes the thoroughly combined or blended preparation of the high priest's grain offering dough. The LXX's pephuramene ("thoroughly kneaded/mixed") confirms the mixing/blending sense. EMET's "soaked" emphasizes the liquid component accurately (the offering is prepared with oil) but loses the thoroughness of the combining action. "Well mixed" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) renders the sense of deliberate, complete combination more completely.

---

### D-LEV006-17 | Verse 21 | tufini minchat pittim — "broken pieces" vs "baked pieces"

**Hebrew:** תֻּפִינֵי מִנְחַת פִּתִּים (tufini minchat pittim)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "broken pieces of the grain offering" |
| LOGOS | "baked pieces like a grain offering" |
| PNEUMA | "baked portions of the grain offering" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET: "broken pieces of the grain offering." The word pittim (from pit, "morsel, piece, crumb") denotes small broken or crumbled pieces, confirmed by the LXX's klasmata ("broken pieces / fragments" — from klao, "to break"). LOGOS and PNEUMA both introduce "baked" for tufini/pittim, but "baked" is not directly in these words; tufini (also rare) describes the form of the offering (crumbled or broken pieces) rather than the method of preparation. The griddle-preparation is already stated in the first half of the verse (al machabat be-shemen, "on a griddle with oil"). EMET's "broken pieces of the grain offering" is the most accurate rendering of the two rare terms.

---

### D-LEV006-18 | Verse 22 | ha-kohen ha-mashiach asher yimashach tachatav mi-banav — describing the successor high priest

**Hebrew:** הַכֹּהֵן הַמָּשִׁיחַ אֲשֶׁר-יִמָּשַׁח תַּחְתָּיו מִבָּנָיו (ha-kohen ha-mashiach asher yimashach tachatav mi-banav)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "the anointed priest who is in his place from his sons" |
| LOGOS | "the priest who is anointed in his place from among his sons" |
| PNEUMA | "Whichever of Aaron's sons is the anointed priest who succeeds him" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET: "the anointed priest who is in his place from his sons." The phrase tachatav ("in his place") expresses dynastic succession — the one who stands in Aaron's place, occupying the same office. EMET's "who is in his place" renders tachatav most literally and preserves the dynastic image. LOGOS's "who is anointed in his place from among his sons" is also accurate and slightly smoother. PNEUMA's "who succeeds him" paraphrases tachatav adequately but introduces "Aaron" explicitly where the MT uses only the pronoun. SOPHIA prefers EMET's rendering for its proximity to the Hebrew construction, with "from his sons" (mi-banav, "from among his sons") as the qualifying phrase.

---

### D-LEV006-19 | Verse 22 | chuqat olam — "perpetual statute" vs "permanent obligation" (second occurrence)

**Hebrew:** חָק-עוֹלָם (chuq olam)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "perpetual statute" |
| LOGOS | "perpetual statute" |
| PNEUMA | "permanent obligation" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET/LOGOS: "perpetual statute." See D-LEV006-13 for reasoning. PNEUMA's "permanent obligation" again captures only the duty side of the formulaic legislative phrase chuqat olam. Consistent with the ruling at v.18.

---

### D-LEV006-20 | Verse 22 | kalil tuqtar — "wholly burned" vs "burned in its entirety" vs "completely burned"

**Hebrew:** כָּלִיל תֻּקְטָר (kalil tuqtar)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "it shall be wholly burned" |
| LOGOS | "it shall be burned in its entirety" |
| PNEUMA | "it is to be completely burned" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET: "it shall be wholly burned." The Hebrew kalil ("whole, entire, complete") functions as an adverb/predicate adjective with the verb tuqtar ("it shall be burned/offered"). "Wholly burned" renders kalil tuqtar as a concise English equivalent — a single adverb modifying the verb. "In its entirety" (LOGOS) is accurate but lengthier; "completely burned" (PNEUMA) conveys the sense but uses "completely" where "wholly" is the more precise liturgical term (corresponding to the LXX's holokautoma and the established sense of kalil in the Levitical system). SOPHIA uses "wholly burned" consistently for kalil throughout (v.22, v.23).

---

### D-LEV006-21 | Verse 25 | chattat — "sin offering" vs "purification offering" (section header, zot torat ha-chattat)

**Hebrew:** זֹאת תּוֹרַת הַחַטָּאת (zot torat ha-chattat)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "This is the instruction of the sin offering." |
| LOGOS | "This is the instruction of the sin offering." |
| PNEUMA | "This is the procedure for the purification offering." |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET/LOGOS: "This is the instruction of the sin offering." The project convention for chattat is "sin offering" (D-LEV005-09; D-LEV006-11). PNEUMA's "purification offering" is again noted and overruled. Additionally, PNEUMA uses "procedure" for torah; the project convention is "instruction."

---

### D-LEV006-22 | Verse 25 | chattat in the sin offering section — slaughter clause

**Hebrew:** תִּשָּׁחֵט הַחַטָּאת לִפְנֵי יְהוָה (tisshachet ha-chattat lifne YHWH)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "the sin offering shall be slaughtered before the LORD" |
| LOGOS | "the sin offering shall be slaughtered before the LORD" |
| PNEUMA | "the purification offering is to be slaughtered before the LORD" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET/LOGOS. See D-LEV006-11, D-LEV006-21. "Sin offering" throughout.

---

### D-LEV006-23 | Verse 26 | ha-kohen asher yechattena — "who offers it as a sin offering" vs "who offers it for sin"

**Hebrew:** הַכֹּהֵן אֲשֶׁר יְחַטְּאֶנָּה (ha-kohen asher yechattena)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "The priest who offers it as a sin offering shall eat it" |
| LOGOS | "The priest who offers it for sin shall eat it" |
| PNEUMA | "The priest who offers it for sin shall eat it" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS/PNEUMA: "The priest who offers it for sin shall eat it." The Hebrew yechattena is a piel of chata — literally "who sin-offers it" or "who performs the sin-offering with it." The piel of chata in this context means "to perform the sin-offering ritual" or "to offer it as a sin offering for sin." LOGOS and PNEUMA's "for sin" is a functional rendering that captures the purpose clause inherent in the piel form. EMET's "offers it as a sin offering" introduces the full technical term in the relative clause in a way that is slightly redundant (the reader already knows the subject is the sin offering from vv.24-25). "Offers it for sin" is cleaner.

---

### D-LEV006-24 | Verse 27 | yizzeh / nizzah — "splashed" vs "sprinkled" vs "spatters"

**Hebrew:** אֲשֶׁר יִזֶּה מִדָּמָהּ (asher yizzeh mi-damah)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "when any of its blood is splashed" |
| LOGOS | "when any of its blood is sprinkled" |
| PNEUMA | "if any of its blood spatters" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET: "splashed." The root n-z-h in the niphal/qal describes an inadvertent scattering of liquid — here blood accidentally contacting a garment during slaughter or blood manipulation. It is critically distinct from the deliberate ritual sprinkling (hizah in the hiphil, Lev 4:6, 17; 5:9), which is an intentional liturgical act. "Splashed" correctly signals the inadvertent, accidental character of the contact. "Sprinkled" (LOGOS) risks confusion with the technical hiphil sprinkling vocabulary. "Spatters" (PNEUMA) is accurate about the inadvertent nature but is more colloquial in register.

---

### D-LEV006-25 | Verse 27 | asher yizzeh alav — rendering of the second blood-contact clause

**Hebrew:** אֲשֶׁר יִזֶּה עָלָיו תְּכַבֵּס בְּמָקוֹם קָדוֹשׁ (asher yizzeh alav tekhabbes bi-maqom qadosh)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "that which was splashed you shall wash in a holy place" |
| LOGOS | "that on which it was sprinkled you shall wash in a holy place" |
| PNEUMA | "that garment must be washed in a holy place" |

**SOPHIA:** Adopts a composite: "that on which it was splashed you shall wash in a holy place." LOGOS's construction "that on which it was [verb]" most precisely renders the relative clause asher yizzeh alav (literally "that upon which [blood] splashed/was splashed"). SOPHIA substitutes EMET's "splashed" (per D-LEV006-24) into LOGOS's structure. PNEUMA's "that garment" simplifies the referent to the antecedent noun (ha-beged, "the garment"), which is acceptable in English but loses the relative clause structure that specifies the garment only insofar as it was contacted by the blood.

---

### D-LEV006-26 | Verse 28 | kli cheres — "earthen vessel" vs "clay vessel"

**Hebrew:** כְּלִי-חֶרֶשׂ (kli cheres)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "earthen vessel" |
| LOGOS | "earthen vessel" |
| PNEUMA | "clay vessel" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET/LOGOS: "earthen vessel." The Hebrew cheres (pottery, earthenware) is rendered "earthen vessel" in the established English liturgical and legal tradition (cf. Lev 11:33; 14:5, 50; Num 5:17). "Earthen" specifies the fired-pottery material (earth-derived, porous) while "clay" is technically the unfired precursor material. In the context of priestly law and vessel-purity regulations, "earthen vessel" is both more accurate and more traditional.

---

### D-LEV006-27 | Verse 28 | ve-shutaf ba-mayim — "rinsed with water" vs "rinsed in water"

**Hebrew:** וְשֻׁטַּף בַּמָּיִם (ve-shutaf ba-mayim)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "rinsed with water" |
| LOGOS | "rinsed in water" |
| PNEUMA | "rinsed in water" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS/PNEUMA: "rinsed in water." The Hebrew ba-mayim ("in the water" or "with water") uses the preposition be-, which covers both "with" and "in." "Rinsed in water" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is the slightly more natural English idiom for the immersion-and-rinse process described. "Rinsed with water" (EMET) is not wrong but "in water" better captures the sense of the vessel being submerged or thoroughly flushed with water during the purification procedure.

---

### D-LEV006-28 | Verse 29 | kol-zakhar ba-kohanim — "every male" vs "any male"

**Hebrew:** כָּל-זָכָר בַּכֹּהֲנִים (kol-zakhar ba-kohanim)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "every male among the priests" |
| LOGOS | "every male among the priests" |
| PNEUMA | "any male among the priests" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET/LOGOS: "every male among the priests." The Hebrew kol ("every/all") expresses totality — the entire class of male priests has this eating right, without exception or selection. "Any" (PNEUMA) introduces a distributive or permissive sense ("any one who wants to"), which understates the comprehensiveness of kol. "Every" preserves the totalizing force of the Hebrew.

---

### D-LEV006-29 | Verse 30 | chattat — "sin offering" vs "purification offering" (final occurrence)

**Hebrew:** וְכָל-חַטָּאת (ve-khol-chattat)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "But no sin offering" |
| LOGOS | "But no sin offering" |
| PNEUMA | "But no purification offering" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET/LOGOS: "But no sin offering." See D-LEV006-11, D-LEV006-21. PNEUMA's "purification offering" overruled. Project convention maintained throughout.

---

### D-LEV006-30 | Verse 30 | asher yuva mi-damo — "from whose blood any was brought" vs "from which any blood was brought"

**Hebrew:** אֲשֶׁר יוּבָא מִדָּמָהּ אֶל-אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (asher yuva mi-damah el-ohel mo'ed)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "from whose blood any was brought into the tent of meeting" |
| LOGOS | "from which any blood is brought into the tent of meeting" |
| PNEUMA | "when its blood has been brought into the tent of meeting" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules LOGOS with a tense adjustment: "from which any blood was brought into the tent of meeting." The relative clause asher yuva mi-damah ("whose blood was brought" — yuva is qal passive of bo, "to come/be brought," here in the perfect or past) refers to a specific completed act of inner-sanctuary blood manipulation. LOGOS's present tense "is brought" works for a general principle but the past tense ("was brought") more precisely indicates the completed act that triggers the no-eating rule. EMET's "from whose blood any was brought" uses a slightly awkward English genitive; LOGOS's "from which any blood was brought" is cleaner. PNEUMA's "when its blood has been brought" uses a temporal clause rather than a relative clause, which paraphrases the structure but not inaccurately.

---

### D-LEV006-31 | Verse 30 | ba-esh tissaref — "shall be burned with fire" vs "must be burned in the fire"

**Hebrew:** בָּאֵשׁ תִּשָּׂרֵף (ba-esh tissaref)

| Translator | Rendering |
|------------|-----------|
| EMET | "it shall be burned with fire" |
| LOGOS | "it shall be burned with fire" |
| PNEUMA | "it must be burned in the fire" |

**SOPHIA:** Rules EMET/LOGOS: "it shall be burned with fire." The Hebrew ba-esh ("with fire" or "in fire") and tissaref (niphal imperfect of saraf, "to burn") form a declaratory legislative statement. The imperfect in the priestly legislative style functions as a directive ("it shall be"), not merely a prediction. EMET/LOGOS preserve this declarative legislative form. PNEUMA's "must" (deontic modal) is not inaccurate but introduces an English obligation marker not present in the Hebrew form. "With fire" (EMET/LOGOS) is the more precise preposition rendering of ba-esh ("in/with fire"; the beth of accompaniment or instrument).
